words: This conversation with TOM WAHL  ELIZABETH GAMBLE WAHL took place at their home
in Pullman, Washington on April 4, 1977. The interviewer was SAM
SCHRAGER.
TOM WAHL: You won't get muchkbecause I can't remember where we were or what. I
think you've about pumped me dry.
SAM SCHRAGER: One thing that I was thinking about was, when you were young and
growing up, how relatively well people were doing around Genesee kno
wing that the area was developed earlier comparatively than the rest of
the county for farming. I wonder how people were doing in the teens
and '20's.

speaker: TW
words: Well, as near as I can tell, of course my memory doesn't start quite

speaker: TW
words: as early as you might wish it would. 1 was born in 1911, as you know.

speaker: TW
words: But when I was about six years old, I think farmers, people that had

speaker: TW
words: come in early and had land and had stuff to sell, I think they were

speaker: TW
words: fairly prosperous by the standards of those times. By that, I mean my

speaker: TW
words: saw fit to buy a Ford car in about 1917,. Model T Ford car, this

speaker: TW
words: was their first automobile, and I suppose there was a little inflation

speaker: TW
words: going on too, because the war was heating up a little later. So in

speaker: TW
words: 1918 they traded that Ford car for etoodge and in 1919 they traded that

speaker: TW
words: Dodge for a Franklin. So you can kind of see a step up in the quality

speaker: TW
words: of the car. However, it might point it up a little better- I did hear

speaker: TW
words: my father make the remark that the Dodge car, he got more for it on the

speaker: TW
words: trade than he had paid for it. There was no such thing as used cars

speaker: TW
words: you know, they were a pretty easy item to sell, but there must have

speaker: TW
words: been some inflation there too. jh doubt there was quite a pad in

speaker: TW
words: the prices. It seemed to me that that Franklin car probably cost about

speaker: TW
words: like a Cadillac would cost, something like that. And I think that he

speaker: TW
words: gave something over $2,700 for it.

speaker: SS
words: Do you think that he paid for that in cash at the time?

speaker: TW
words: Oh, I think so. Course, he might have had to borrow the money at a

speaker: TW
words: bank, but I don't think he ever bought anything on time, time payments.

speaker: SS
words: You say inflation, makes me think of the- you said he never would have

speaker: SS
words: bought anything on time?

speaker: TW
words: No. Finance it though a bank. You know, pay as you go hadn't really

speaker: TW
words: started then, either. You had credit at the bank, you had credit at

speaker: TW
words: the stores, and it was customary for farmers to pay their bills once a

speaker: TW
words: people vuher

speaker: TW
words: year- after harvest. It was a farm oriented.economy. And the farmers

speaker: TW
words: did business, they expected to carry them til after harvest. But

speaker: TW
words: probably he bought these things after harvest. Well, come to think of

speaker: TW
words: it, he didn't buy the Ford after harvest, because he bought it early

speaker: TW
words: in the summer of 1917.

speaker: SS
words: Do you remember the prices as being particularly good?

speaker: TW
words: I canrt tell you what the prices for farm produce were, but compared to

speaker: TW
words: what you buy, I think they probably were pretty good. Now my father

speaker: TW
words: told of when he first came there, and that's before there was a rail

speaker: TW
words: road at Genesee, they hauled their wheat to Dewiston in those days.

speaker: TW
words: And that would be some time after 1880; before my time. Haul a whole

speaker: TW
words: load of wheat to Lewiston and only have enough money to buy a pair of

speaker: TW
words: cowhide boots. So there was a time before that that had been really

speaker: TW
words: tough. And it was really tough, too, from what he has said. Now, I

speaker: TW
words: think in maybe 1890- I'm guessing here a little bit- but by 1890, I

speaker: TW
words: think things had gotten quite a bit better. But in the year of 1893

speaker: TW
words: they had a wet year and hardly anybody harvested anything in this whole

speaker: TW
words: country, this whole Palouse country down there. They didn't have the

speaker: TW
words: machinery to thresh with that they have now. They didn't have the

speaker: TW
words: machinery to handle it with. .people headed in that time.

speaker: TW
words: And a header, as you probably know, is a machine that just cuts wheat

speaker: TW
words: about as a combine does, but doesn't thresh it, you have to then put

speaker: TW
words: it in a stationary threshing machine or in a stack and later a thre

speaker: TW
words: shing machine. He passed up a chance I guess to get his threshed; it

speaker: TW
words: was headed already, that is, cut and stacked and he passed up a chance

speaker: TW
words: to get it threshed because he was kind of hoping for a better deal on

speaker: TW
words: the threshing. And it started raining and nobody that hadn't done it

speaker: TW
words: real early, nobody got their crop harvested. And he said that except

speaker: TW
words: for the fact that he had- that the merchants and bankers carried them

speaker: TW
words: for the next year, it would have been awful tough going because they

speaker: TW
words: didn't have any money. Of course, they were not as dependent on money

speaker: TW
words: then as you are now. There was very little taxes and very few things

speaker: TW
words: you had to buy, most of the stuff you produced and canned it, preser

speaker: TW
words: ved it, in one way or another.

speaker: SS
words: What about the role of J. P. at that time?

speaker: TW
words: Itrs all kind of hearsay as far as I'm concerned, but there was an awful

speaker: TW
words: lot of land passed into hands, and I think most of it through

speaker: TW
words: foreclosure of mortgages.

speaker: TW
words: What do you know about his background? How did he come in there? Can

speaker: TW
words: you tell me that from what you've heard?

speaker: SS
words: What I've heard that it was largely through Lewiston- that he came to

speaker: SS
words: Lewiston. And I've heard all kind of stories that he made quite a bit

speaker: SS
words: of money on the reservation land. He sold whiskey to the Indians and

speaker: SS
words: various things like that which may or may not be true.

speaker: TW
words: Did he come in there and start out running a saloon? Do you know that?

speaker: SS
words: In Lewiston?

speaker: TW
words: Uh-huh.

speaker: SS
words: Well, in connection with selling whiskey- I've heard about his early

speaker: SS
words: activities- whether he had a saloon—

speaker: TW
words: I think I heard someone make this crack, that he came in there with

speaker: TW
words: nothing but a bottle of whiskey and a box of cigars. So he didn't

speaker: TW
words: start at the top of the totem pole.

speaker: SS
words: By 1890 he certainly had quite a bit of^townsyon the railroad.

speaker: TW
words: There is this that I've heard, and I think it's true enough; he seemed

speaker: TW
words: very much to have his name perpetuated. And he started, somehow or

speaker: TW
words: other a number of little towns by either contributing the land or some

speaker: TW
words: thing with the stipulation that the town would be called Volmer. And

speaker: TW
words: there was one over near Troy, you know, and I think there was one or

speaker: TW
words: two up on the Reservation. And every time he did this, so the story

speaker: TW
words: goes, the first act of the town meeting was to change the name of the

speaker: TW
words: town to something else. Now wasn't Troy Volmer?

speaker: SS
words: Yes, that was the original name.

speaker: TW
words: I think there was one or two over in Nez Perce County.

speaker: SS
words: Well who carried your parents at that time?

speaker: SS
words: Genesee there in my memory was a branch of the Lewiston bank. And

speaker: TW
words: Well, I suppose they bankedA the Volmer bank. That is, the bank at

speaker: TW
words: there was two banks there when I was- you, know, within my memory.

speaker: TW
words: One was the Genesee Exchange Bank and the other was the First Bank of

speaker: TW
words: Genesee. And I think that the First Bank of Genesee was a branch of

speaker: TW
words: the Volmer bank at Lewiston. And the Exchange Bank, I think was lo-

speaker: TW
words: cally owned. The Exchange Bank went broke in my memory, during the

speaker: TW
words: Depression, the other one didn't.

speaker: SS
words: Was there still hard feelings when you were growing up about Volmer

speaker: SS
words: and the Volmer bank.

speaker: TW
words: Oh, I think so. Of course, you know, farmers typically are sort of

speaker: TW
words: mad at their bankers, or were. But this bank, they were particularly

speaker: TW
words: there was a particular amount of hard feeling between the farmers and

speaker: TW
words: the bank, Dont you think, Liz? Vollmer burnt

speaker: TW
words: ELIZABETH WAHL: Yes.

speaker: TW
words: Now there was a man named White and-

speaker: EW
words: And he was highly disliked, I know that.

speaker: TW
words: White worked for J. C. Volmer. And I don't know just what

speaker: TW
words: his duties were, but it's been referred to many times that he was sort

speaker: TW
words: of, oh, he did some of the unpleasant things for him. He was not an

speaker: TW
words: attractive looking man, but I don't think he was too bad of a man, but

speaker: TW
words: he was a sort of what I've heard him referred to as a scalper. And

speaker: TW
words: this is not anything really illegal, but- let's see if I could remember

speaker: TW
words: this incident- Yes. He acquired a cloud on the title of a piece of

speaker: TW
words: property that my father owned, I guess, and it cost my dad some money

speaker: TW
words: to get it straightened out. And that was a sort of a way of S. T. White

speaker: TW
words: sort of making his living, I guess. Although the families were friend

speaker: TW
words: ly, still my father always held that against him. It wasn't a big things

speaker: TW
words: it wasn't a thing that you couldn't live with, but it was a thing that

speaker: TW
words: he resented,

speaker: SS
words: Did it as an agent of Volmer? Do you think? Or just on his own?

speaker: TW
words: He was in a position to do this probably because he knew things working

speaker: TW
words: for Volmer that he might not otherwise have known. But, no, I don't

speaker: TW
words: think that the bank itself was involved in this.

speaker: SS
words: Volmerrs presence continued to be felt around Genesee long after the

speaker: SS
words: '93 depression.

speaker: TW
words: You take the book like this Atlas here, and you go through it and you

speaker: TW
words: can just see an awful lot of farm land listed in the name of J. P. Vollmer

speaker: TW
words: mer. Now, land wasn't worth much, and times had been hard and a lot of

speaker: TW
words: people quit when they didn't have to quit, and I don't think there'd

speaker: TW
words: be anything illegal about it, but a lot of people felt like- particularly

speaker: TW
words: the farmers- feel like if he got you in a bind, why he

speaker: TW
words: wouldn't back off.

speaker: SS
words: Do you think it happened mostly through the store, through his general

speaker: SS
words: merchandise store, and those kind of loans? Or through the bank?

speaker: TW
words: My knowledge would have been more through the bank.

speaker: SS
words: Mortgages and things?

speaker: TW
words: Mortgages. Yes, uh-huh. And through the panics and depressions of

speaker: TW
words: the time,

speaker: SS
words: Do you think that one year was enough to-

speaker: TW
words: I'll bet it's more than one year, because I don't think prosperity

speaker: TW
words: started very early. I know my people had it really pretty hard through

speaker: TW
words: the '80's. I don't believe that their prosperity started til maybe just

speaker: TW
words: before '93- '93 probably wiped out what prosperity they had, and then

speaker: TW
words: probably beginning 1894- as near as I can tell there must have been

speaker: TW
words: quite a little bit of prosperity then from about 1895 on up into and

speaker: TW
words: through the '20's. At least, there were better houses, you know, and

speaker: TW
words: of course there's lots of things to spend money for, too. Fences and

speaker: TW
words: everything else. Machinery. It was the beginning of the mechanization

speaker: TW
words: of the country.

speaker: SS
words: What about the Rosenstein store?

speaker: TW
words: Well, my parents liked the Rosensteins very, very well. And, of course

speaker: TW
words: and like all the other merchants I think they put out credit for a year.

speaker: TW
words: I don't think anybody did cash business with the farmers. My dad, when

speaker: TW
words: the stores started doing it every month he resented it heartily. Just

speaker: TW
words: wasn't cricket at all.

speaker: SS
words: Did you know the Rosensteins at all?

speaker: SS
words: always gave me a cookie. They owned some land right close to where we

speaker: TW
words: Yes, I can remember them. I liked to go in there, too, because they

speaker: TW
words: farmed.

speaker: SS
words: How much do you think they were part of being Jewish

speaker: SS
words: people? They were somewhat set apart?

speaker: TW
words: Well, I don't know. I suppose, people being as they are, there always

speaker: TW
words: was a discrimination by some people of almost any minority that you'd

speaker: TW
words: care to name. But it was common knowledge that the Rosensteins were

speaker: TW
words: Jewish and most people seemed to think quite highly of them. They

speaker: TW
words: weren't the only Jewish people there. I don't know when the Casebolts

speaker: TW
words: came. I they were Jewish.

speaker: SS
words: And the Levis, I have heard.

speaker: TW
words: Yes. I didn't know them, but I've heard the name.

speaker: SS
words: How much of an influence in the development of the area do you think

speaker: SS
words: that the opening of the Reservation land was in f$5? Just in the tra

speaker: SS
words: ditions that you've heard about it.

speaker: TW
words: Well, now, I couldn't tell you, I just don't know. My parents bought

speaker: TW
words: Indian land later, and really historically I don't know too much what

speaker: TW
words: the opening of the Reservation entailed, but a lot of Indians sold

speaker: TW
words: their land- patented their land and sold it in order to get money.

speaker: TW
words: A lot of them didn't. There's a lot of land still held by Indians,

speaker: TW
words: too, on the Reservation there. I suppose that much fine farm land

speaker: TW
words: being open to ahe Indians, being opened for farming, probably made quite

speaker: TW
words: a difference. I know a lot of people that were established over near

speaker: TW
words: Moscow- well, I shouldn't say I know a lot of people- but some people

speaker: TW
words: went over and homesteaded over in the Camas Prairie area. Now I don't

speaker: TW
words: know how much of that had been Reservation land. What did they do?

speaker: TW
words: Did they open the Reservation to homesteading?

speaker: SS
words: Yeah, in '95. First they distributed the land to those Indians, Nez

speaker: SS
words: Perces, that were there, I don't know exactly what the amount was, but

speaker: SS
words: after they had given them a certain allotment, then they threw the

speaker: SS
words: whole thing open.

speaker: TW
words: I see.

speaker: SS
words: Some of what was left. I've seen the old Lewiston Tribune. The day

speaker: SS
words: it was opened and it was a stampede.

speaker: TW
words: Well, that probably explains how come that I had an uncle that homesteaded

speaker: TW
words: over near Ferdinand. And my father, you understand, never homesteaded.

speaker: TW
words: His father homesteaded and all of his brothers aid sisters

speaker: TW
words: homesteaded some place or other, but he didn't. He was 'the oldest sur-

speaker: TW
words: viving son after his brother died and it was necessary for one mature

speaker: TW
words: man to be sort of in charge of the father's affairs. See there was

speaker: TW
words: the widow and all the kids to take care of and he was the oldest and

speaker: TW
words: there was, I think, twelve kids. And, let's see, my dad was ten years

speaker: TW
words: old, I guess, when they they came there- he was born in '70- and he

speaker: TW
words: was about twenty years old when his father died- eighteen.

speaker: SS
words: And he took over the family?

speaker: TW
words: Well, his brother- he had a brother that was a couple of years older

speaker: TW
words: but he got killed a couple of years later, I guess.

speaker: EW
words: He was eighteen, I believe, when his brother oW/ntd. He was born in 1870.

speaker: EW
words: Very noisy- some of the next few sentences were obliterated.

speaker: TW
words: , my dad was born in 1870, February 5, 1870. His father died in

speaker: TW
words: 1887, leaving John, the oldest son in charge of the place there. But

speaker: TW
words: John died in 1893. So my father would have been at that time- would

speaker: TW
words: have been twenty-three years old.

speaker: SS
words: Did he have many brothers and sisters in the family?

speaker: TW
words: They would, of course, have all been younger. And one of 'em was an

speaker: TW
words: epileptic, and that putlthe others quite a bit younger and quite a few

speaker: TW
words: of them were girls. But all of them, I think, with the possible excep

speaker: TW
words: tion of the epileptic homesteaded some land somewhere. And two of them

speaker: TW
words: went to college, which my father helped them do; helped them go to

speaker: TW
words: college. And he always felt like he had given up his chance for an

speaker: TW
words: education by doing that.

speaker: SS
words: Did it cause him to delay his marriage at all?

speaker: TW
words: Well, yes. Let's see, my dad didn't marry until 1907. So that would

speaker: TW
words: have made him 37 years old.

speaker: SS
words: I wonder if that child that was epileptic, I wonder if that was the

speaker: SS
words: one that Mary Edwards mentioned to me?

speaker: TW
words: She probably knew about it, but I doubt if she knew him. Let's see, he

speaker: TW
words: died- I don't have the date of his death. No, I don't have the date of

speaker: TW
words: his death.

speaker: SS
words: Was it your mother or your grandmother that she adored so much?

speaker: TW
words: My mother. She was probably talking about my brother, who was a crip

speaker: TW
words: ple, but he wasn^t epileptic.

speaker: EW
words: He was spastic, Tom's brother was spastic.

speaker: SS
words: She told me that she thought so highly of your mother. She just adored

speaker: SS
words: her.

speaker: TW
words: Well, my mother thought a great deal of all of the Gr«y children, too.

speaker: TW
words: Course my mother was the young matron of the community and they were the

speaker: TW
words: kids, you know, of her friends- of friends that were older, of course

speaker: TW
words: than my people. And they were our closest neighbors, Mary and her bro

speaker: TW
words: thers. And, well, her brothers, at least some of them sort of started

speaker: TW
words: their first job working for my dad. And this young couple, I think

speaker: TW
words: they were quite pleased to have these nice, intelligent, young kids

speaker: TW
words: in their house.

speaker: SS
words: I'm sure she's told me how she would love to come over and play the

speaker: SS
words: piano with your mother.

speaker: TW
words: Mother was a teacher, and I think by instinct a teacher too. Wasn't

speaker: TW
words: she Liz?

speaker: EW
words: Oh, yes. Pretty good teacher.

speaker: TW
words: She couldn't help but try to teach anybody that came within her reach.

speaker: SS
words: That would explain why she she said she'd rather been at your house

speaker: SS
words: than anywhere.

speaker: TW
words: Uh-huh. Probably.

speaker: EW
words: And Tom's mother taught her how to sew.

speaker: TW
words: And Mary and her sister and her brothers, of course they were all ol

speaker: TW
words: der than I was but they were always real nice to me. You know, older

speaker: TW
words: kids can make it kind of miserable for a younger kid or they can make

speaker: TW
words: it pretty nice. Well, they made it pretty nice for me. And one of

speaker: TW
words: the nice things they did- these kids had an awful lot of freedom, even

speaker: TW
words: for these kind of times, and one thing they had was a coaster wagon

speaker: TW
words: and an old gray horse. And they hitched the singletree to this horse

speaker: TW
words: to this little kids wagon and dragged it back and forth. See, they

speaker: TW
words: farmed on each side of us, and so sometimes there was cooking going on

speaker: TW
words: down at this place about two or three miles below or south of us, or the

speaker: TW
words: cooking was going on at their place about three-quarters of a mile north

speaker: TW
words: of us. So every day,maybe several times a day, these kids'd make this

speaker: TW
words: trip with the old gray horse and the kid's wagon. So I got to ride in

speaker: TW
words: this wagon once in a while.

speaker: SS
words: You say the cooking was going on.

speaker: TW
words: Well, where ever they were working, you know, why, probably their mo

speaker: TW
words: ther would be cooking the meals at one place or the other. Because,

speaker: TW
words: you know, with horses you don't jump in the car and drive from where

speaker: TW
words: you feed the horses, you don't go home every time. If you're working

speaker: TW
words: in the field this far away, you " arrange to live there somehow

speaker: TW
words: or other. I think really, how came these two farms- well, now, the

speaker: TW
words: Gray homestead was down there and then one of the Gr^y boys when he

speaker: TW
words: got older bought a place between the homestead and our place. And then

speaker: TW
words: there was the land to the north of us: I don't know how they acquired

speaker: TW
words: it, but as far back as my memory reaches and before that, they had this

speaker: TW
words: land on both sides of us.

speaker: TW
words: that

speaker: SS
words: The average farm^wasn't split though, you wouldn't be doing that, you

speaker: SS
words: would have to be cooking out in the fields, would you? Or would people

speaker: SS
words: still ?

speaker: TW
words: Well, I'll tell you how we farmed. We had a farm in Nez Perce County

speaker: TW
words: and a farm in Latah County and they were three or four miles apart,

speaker: TW
words: and there was a barn and my father built a house- a barn- this was

speaker: TW
words: on the Reservation, three or four miles from where his home was, and

speaker: TW
words: when they were farming down there they just simply moved down there.

speaker: TW
words: Or, sometimes his brother would farm down there and one of his sisters

speaker: TW
words: would cook down at this other place. Later he rented it, they kind of

speaker: TW
words: split up and they had two outfits to farm with then and it wasn't necessary

speaker: TW
words: to all this moving back and forth. No, with horses, when

speaker: TW
words: you moved down to one farm you moved your horses there and stayed there

speaker: TW
words: and took care of them. And you know, you couldn't quickly move from one

speaker: TW
words: place to the other. It might take you, well, to go down four or five

speaker: TW
words: miles and get your horses hooked up and do any work and get unhookedwith

speaker: TW
words: horses, you just/can't take your lunch either, you've got to stop

speaker: TW
words: and feed the horses. It takes you an hour and a half for noon, you

speaker: TW
words: know. You're working but the horses aren't. You come in about eleven

speaker: TW
words: thirty and you've got all those horses to feed before you get your food

speaker: TW
words: and then you've got to hook 'em up again, see that they're watered.

speaker: TW
words: And night you've got to feed 'em when you bring 'em in from the field

speaker: TW
words: and unharness them and after supper you've got to go out and feed 'em

speaker: TW
words: again and in the morning you've got to get up and harness them and

speaker: TW
words: feed them and then after breakfast you've got to go out. to the imple

speaker: TW
words: ment you're pulling. It's a lot different from running a tractor.

speaker: TW
words: So, you can't- well, just to give you an idea; now my father didn't

speaker: TW
words: T particularly hard, he didn't work his men particularly hard by the

speaker: TW
words: standards of those times, but even as a kid. I was running a team for

speaker: TW
words: 'em- I had to get up at five-thirty'd be the latest, and you'd go out

speaker: TW
words: and feed and harness your horses and clean out your barn. There was

speaker: TW
words: all that manure, too, you know that's got to be pitched out. And then

speaker: TW
words: come back in and eat and then go back out and get 'em strung out and

speaker: TW
words: you were expected to be in the field with that team by seven; not

speaker: TW
words: eight or nine, but seven o'clock! Or at the very latest seven-thirty.

speaker: TW
words: But when you consider that you gotta stop by eleven-thirty, why you

speaker: TW
words: don't get an awful lot of plowing done. And when you consider that

speaker: TW
words: the outfit that one man drove usually consisted of two fourteen-inch

speaker: TW
words: plows and eight head of horses and compare thatwwith what a tractor

speaker: TW
words: can do

speaker: TW
words: — plowing 160 acres. You spent all summer, near, it seemed like.

speaker: SS
words: That would mean then a good long period of time at the other place

speaker: SS
words: when you were down there?

speaker: TW
words: Yeah, and you'd be- Well, farming- what you would do then- you did

speaker: TW
words: what farming you could do, and you got done what you could do. And

speaker: TW
words: it depended on the weather as it does now, but more so. And in the

speaker: TW
words: spring, as early as you possibly could you'd probably start plowing,

speaker: TW
words: and if you could get some of that work done and seeded, if you felt

speaker: TW
words: like you had to you might, but most likely you would seed ground that

speaker: TW
words: you had plowed the previous fall if you was going to put in any spring

speaker: TW
words: crop. But we, in my youth, very little spring crops that we put in

speaker: TW

speaker: EW
words: I avoided much disciplinary trouble by playing with the children.

speaker: EW
words: And playing all the games. I was not a good baseball player, but I

speaker: EW
words: could play all the games like, oh, darebase and prisoners base and

speaker: EW
words: any of those games where you ran and had fun. It was also good for

speaker: EW
words: the teacher as well as good for the children. Gave you your exercise!

speaker: EW
words: Gave you.exercise and you let down the bars for a little while and

speaker: EW
words: just played as one of them. Then when the bell rang things went

speaker: EW
words: right back into their ordinary position

speaker: SS
words: You mean when you were on the playground with the kids you were no

speaker: SS
words: longer the teacher?

speaker: EW
words: Yes I was in a way. But, still I would take my place- take a place

speaker: EW
words: the kids would chose me just as if I were another child and we played

speaker: EW
words: together and - they might want me to settle dis

speaker: EW
words: putes and I would do that.

speaker: SS
words: But you certainly didn't idscipline or you didn't boss them around.

speaker: EW
words: No. no, no. We all let down and says, "Come on, come on, we'll play."

speaker: EW
words: And they always loved to have the teacher play. Even the big ones

speaker: EW
words: loved to have the teacher play.

speaker: EW
words: — country school, it was. Although this man teacher that I had did

speaker: EW
words: not play with us. The lady before did not play with us, but the lady

speaker: EW
words: following the man teacher, she came out on the playground and she

speaker: EW
words: played largely with the smaller children. The games that you play in

speaker: EW
words: circles and sing. She was a good singer. She sang she didn't play

speaker: EW
words: so much the games of competition; more the circle games. And the year

speaker: EW
words: when I was in the eighth grade we had a teacher^ was real good to

speaker: EW
words: play with us. Real fast runner and play. all the competitive games.

speaker: SS
words: It does to me seem that for a lot of the country girls it was kind of

speaker: SS
words: a chance to get off of the farm to go and train to become a teacher.

speaker: EW
words: Yes, that's true. And with the ones that took very little training

speaker: EW
words: it was a method of making a little money and bring it back to the home,

speaker: EW
words: too. Because that was a very difficult thing in the early days, was

speaker: EW
words: to make a living. And that's why, these men teachers that I mentioned

speaker: EW
words: they had to support their families that way, and a way to help support

speaker: EW
words: the family.

speaker: SS
words: But you would say that a lot of that money the teacher made went to

speaker: SS
words: the family instead of into her own pocket.

speaker: EW
words: Yes, yea. Now that's not true of all of them. But with a great

speaker: EW
words: many it was. But I do know some girls who were such spendthrifts

speaker: EW
words: and had such beautiful clothing and things that their parents even

speaker: EW
words: had to help them. They couldn't make it on their own with their job!

speaker: SS
words: Even as teachers?

speaker: EW
words: Yes. I think that the first teachers that I've heard of at

speaker: EW
words: got about forty dollars a month and then the teaching would only be

speaker: EW
words: maybe three months and then another period of three months. It would

speaker: EW
words: n't be more than six months. And then in later years when they got up

speaker: EW
words: to $100 a month and over $100 a month, that was very good.

speaker: SS
words: Do you think this playing on the playground with the kids made them

speaker: SS
words: lose their fear of the teacher?

speaker: EW
words: Yes, it made them- I don t know, it was very good. And some would say

speaker: EW
words: that you should keep yourself aloof and not be one of them, but no,

speaker: EW
words: you find your children minded you better had much better discipline

speaker: EW
words: if they got acquainted with you well on the schoolground. And then you

speaker: EW
words: came in, you just old 'em if anybody started thinking it was still play

speaker: EW
words: time, told 'em, this is school. And then they have to buckle right

speaker: EW
words: down again. But it: improved the discipline. Kept all these fights

speaker: EW
words: from getting started out on the schoolgrounds.

speaker: SS
words: Did you ever hit kids when you taught? Did you have to do that?

speaker: EW
words: No.

speaker: SS
words: You never did?

speaker: EW
words: Never used any hitting or spanking.

speaker: SS
words: Did you threaten them?

speaker: EW
words: Well, maybe about once. (Laughter) But I never had to spank them. Of

speaker: EW
words: course I had great backing from all the parents. They just backed me

speaker: EW
words: on everything I tried to do. And that makes such a differenc e.

speaker: SS
words: Do you really think that a teacher really brought culture to the com

speaker: SS
words: munity that she taught in? Or is that just kind of a myth?

speaker: EW
words: I think that in the early days they certainly did. And the schools

speaker: EW
words: that I taught in, the people there were already quite cultured people.

speaker: EW
words: I taught at Mt. Tomr and many parents there who had been teachers and

speaker: EW
words: many who were well educated and many, if not so well educated, were

speaker: EW
words: very intelligent.  They were exceptional people. And I don t think I

speaker: EW
words: could have taught them very much that they already didn't have. But

speaker: EW
words: still I could bring out things; for instance there were quite a few

speaker: EW
words: Norwegian children there and I could bring some of the customs of their

speaker: EW
words: country and what their fathers and mothers had done, and that was a

speaker: EW
words: kind of a contribution, you know.

speaker: SS
words: You mean, you taught them respect for their own culture?

speaker: EW
words: Yes. And for children who were not of that background why, that gave

speaker: EW
words: them some idea of how things were done in jdifferent country . And

speaker: EW
words: I know that I was in the fourth grade- we had a teacher there who bro

speaker: EW
words: ught us things that I hadn't had. She sang very well and played the

speaker: EW
words: reed organ; taught us many, many nice songs; children's songs, you

speaker: EW
words: know, and we sang and sang. And she taught us a little botany. None

speaker: EW
words: of us had had any botany, she taught us the parts of flowers and the

speaker: EW
words: names of flowers. She taught us besides music and botany she taught

speaker: EW
words: us art, she taught us quite a little art that we never had any chance

speaker: EW
words: at. So she indeed brought culture with her into the community.

speaker: SS
words: Was she older than most teachers?

speaker: EW
words: No. No, I believe she was eighteen or not more than twenty and she

speaker: EW
words: had attended the normal school.

speaker: SS
words: And so it was from normal school that she got that?

speaker: EW
words: She came from the normal school; I imagine she got a lo t of it in

speaker: EW
words: her home. She probably had music lessons when she was a little girl.

speaker: EW
words: But she sang and she played.

speaker: SS
words: What about the community at large, I mean beyond the children themselves;

speaker: SS
words: now TomfclT like you say, the people were kind of cultured.

speaker: SS
words: But was it the same other places too? I've heard in some places that

speaker: SS
words: the schoolteacher really was a resource person for the community.

speaker: EW
words: Well, I imagine that would be in earlier days then when I was doing

speaker: EW
words: the teaching. I taught also at a school called Lenville and there

speaker: EW
words: in that community. produced America s John Mc Cormick and that was

speaker: EW
words: a young man named Alfred Brigham and he was a grand singer, learned

speaker: EW
words: at the University of Idaho. He went to Chicago and sang there in com

speaker: EW
words: petition; fifteen western states and Canada sent singers and he was

speaker: EW
words: this lovely tenor and he was named America's John Mc Cormick and first

speaker: EW
words: tenor. The next year this minister of music from Spokane sang in the

speaker: EW
words: same competition. He was a baritone and he was named second best bari

speaker: EW
words: tone in this same group. So you see we had quite talented people down

speaker: EW
words: in that part of the country. And there was a man there who had been

speaker: EW
words: a legislator down to Boise, lived in the Lenville area. So, I don't

speaker: EW
words: know-

speaker: SS
words: It's interesting to me what you say about the culture of these people

words: because it seems like they were putting themselves in a much more dif

speaker: SS
words: ficult situation economically compared to perhaps that they had grown

speaker: SS
words: up with- their parents. You know what I mean?

speaker: EW
words: Yes.

speaker: SS
words: You grow up in a cultured home, here they were out on the raw, new

speaker: SS
words: land that had much more limited cultural opportunities, wouldn't that

speaker: SS
words: be so?

speaker: EW
words: Yes. That is so. And I think they made quite a contribution there,

speaker: EW
words: and I know from the college they used to send out a lady who was

speaker: EW
words: head of the music department. She'd come out and sing in the country

speaker: EW
words: schools. And my superintendent, Miss Ellen Peterson, would come out

speaker: EW
words: and play the reed organ. We'd have " in the evenings and sing.

speaker: EW
words: And then there was a man who taught what he called a business college

speaker: EW
words: in Moscow. And through the wintertime he would have night schools at

speaker: EW
words: schoolhouses, and the farmers, adult men all would come there and he

speaker: EW
words: would teach them forms of math that they could use on the farm, you

speaker: EW
words: know, how to figure how much hay there was in a haystack and how to

speaker: EW
words: figure how much there was in a cord of wood and all these things that

speaker: EW
words: were very, very useful to them. And how to write a business letter.

speaker: EW
words: His name was Crick' and he ran Crickner's Business College in Moscow

speaker: EW
words: years ago. Then he would do this teaching at nights of the adult men.

speaker: EW
words: Also the University of Idaho would send out to some of the country

speaker: EW
words: schools the home ec lady and she would teach sewing. Maybe that would

speaker: EW
words: be on a Saturday and the women would come to the schoolhouse. So you

speaker: EW
words: see the schoolhouse was really quite the community center.

speaker: SS
words: Would this be when you were growing up?

speaker: EW
words: No, this would be when I was teaching.

speaker: SS
words: That the women would come out.

speaker: EW
words: Right, right. Well, I guess a little before I was teaching too.

speaker: SS
words: To the adult women rather than to the girls?

speaker: EW
words: Yes. Yes, that's right.

speaker: SS

speaker: EW
words: No, no, there wasn't. It was being done before I was growing up and

speaker: EW
words: it's being done again now.

speaker: SS
words: When you were a child do you remember—?

speaker: EW
words: Well, my mother never did it. But I think there was some quilting and

speaker: EW
words: a 1ot of tying of quilts. Instead of all these little small stitches

speaker: EW
words: they would tie quilts. And that was rather common when I was a girl.

speaker: EW
words: And now the making of quilts is right back ' the swing again!

speaker: SS

words: knew in Victoria.

words: Well, that's true.

words: the rural areas here?

words: year that she was there- you see she came in 1889- and the first year

words: she was there she saw only one white woman, and she came riding^on

speaker: EW
words: horseback, otherwise any women she saw were Indian women. So then you

speaker: EW
words: would miss quite a bit, although the Indian women had their own cul

speaker: EW
words: ture and they brought for her. moccasins for all her boys. Had made

speaker: EW
words: moccasins. She could have learned bead work and things like that from

speaker: EW
words: them, but they didn't even speakAEnglish very well.

speaker: SS

speaker: SS
words: attitudes that people had in those days towards the Indians, how they

speaker: SS
words: looked at them. What do you think?

speaker: EW
words: In the time my mother- now this would be before I was born- when

speaker: EW
words: she just had four little boys, she was just a little bit afraid of them,

speaker: EW
words: A little bit afraid. But by the time I was in the grades I would see

speaker: EW
words: them at the fairs and riding through the country and we accepted them

speaker: EW
words: very well.

speaker: SS
words: Do you think, now I know that this is kind of a hard qu estion, do you

speaker: SS
words: think that the opinion of people at that time was that they were in

speaker: SS
words: ferior?

speaker: EW
words: That they were inferior?

speaker: SS
words: Yes.

speaker: EW
words: Well, no, I don't believe- it was not with my family, anyway. And at

speaker: EW
words: the Mount Tomer school there was a girl went there for a while

speaker: EW
words: was part Indian and she was a very good student and got along just fine.

speaker: EW
words: Pretty well accepted.

speaker: SS
words: The mixing though, there wasn't too much, was there? And it seems like

speaker: SS
words: most of the Indians did stay down on the —

speaker: EW
words: Yes, this girl was a home, sort of an adopted home. And was

speaker: EW
words: not with her parents, and she had come from Lewiston and was helping

speaker: EW
words: an elderly lady there with her work and then coming to school every

speaker: EW
words: day. Fifth or sixth grader. And was company too for this lady.

speaker: SS
words: I was asking about the attitude the people had towards the Indians

speaker: SS
words: in the early days and what they felt about them.

speaker: TW
words: Well, I think my dad's feeling toward them in general was really pret

speaker: TW
words: ty good. Quite a bit of respect for them. Course, I think any person

speaker: TW
words: probably would be struck by the difference in their feelings towards

speaker: TW
words: certain problems compared, you know, two different social cultures,

speaker: TW
words: look at human problems quite differently sometimes. And this struck

speaker: TW
words: him, but he thought quite highly of the Nez Perce Indians here. And

speaker: TW
words: so highly that possibly if there'd been no country schoolteachers- if

speaker: TW
words: the country schoolteachers had all been men, I wouldn't be surprised

speaker: TW
words: if I'd turned to be half Indian! (Laughter)

speaker: TW
words: This diary here contains a trip through Canada looking at land for a

speaker: TW
words: group of farmers in this country and the partner that he had with him

speaker: TW
words: on this trip was married to an Indian and Dad was very fond of this

speaker: TW
words: man's daughters, so that fits right into your question.

speaker: SS
words: Was this afer he lived in - well, it would have been after-

speaker: TW
words: This was before he met my mother sometime; well, it was 19- well,

speaker: TW
words: it wasn't before he met my mother, it was 1905 and he married her in

speaker: TW
words: 1907, so he'd known her a while. But he had also known this man a long

speaker: TW
words: time too.

speaker: SS
words: Did you feel in general the community- was there any looking down at

speaker: SS
words: the mixed marriages at that time?

speaker: TW
words: There was by some people. And it was resented by some people and it

speaker: TW
words: was- I don't know. I went to school with people that were of mixed

speaker: TW
words: parentage. We thought real highly of them. One of the most popular

speaker: TW
words: families in our neighborhood was a mixed marriage and I think the peo

speaker: TW
words: ple felt more highly of the Indian side of the family than the other.

speaker: TW
words: Not because the man had married an Indian, just felt like the Indian

speaker: TW
words: family was kind of their people, nicer people, easier to get along with,

speaker: TW
words: There was this thing that I think probably led to a lot of this feeling

speaker: TW
words: that you expressed was the fact that the Indians did have this land,

speaker: TW
words: and a white man that married an Indian that had a nice allotment of

speaker: TW
words: land had an easier deal than the white man that married a white woman.

speaker: TW
words: And ^ think there was jealousy because of this difference right there,

speaker: TW
words: this money thing. There's always jealousy if the other guy has made

speaker: TW
words: some money and you haven't. And I think that might have had as much

speaker: TW
words: to do with it as anything else. But some of the Indians were highly

speaker: TW
words: thought of. Quite capable people. They had different values, you know,

speaker: TW
words: they didn't drive as hard for financial success. They valued some

speaker: TW
words: other things too. And eventually I think practically all the Indians

speaker: TW
words: have turned out to - found it to their advantage to their land

speaker: TW
words: out to white farmers, although there are some that farmed their own

speaker: TW
words: land. In the early days a lot of them did, but as things got more

speaker: TW
words: complicated, I guess, they didn't do that so much.

speaker: SS
words: When you say they valued other things, what are you thinking of?

speaker: TW
words: Well, an Indian would think Nothing of when it was time to go dig

speaker: TW
words: camas or hunt deer or go to a rodeo or a ballgame, they're quite ath

speaker: TW
words: letic- you know, quite interested in athletics-think toothing of lea

speaker: TW
words: ving his farm at no matter what critical a time and going and taking

speaker: TW
words: his day off. That is, I think they were aware that financially they

speaker: TW
words: wouldn't be ahead, but they also think that they'd rather do this other

speaker: TW
words: thing and do it! (Chuckles)

speaker: SS
words: What about in mixing at dances and that kind of thing? Was there any

speaker: SS
words: taboo about dancing?

speaker: TW
words: Not at our dances. Not in our district. Of course there were only

speaker: TW
words: two families that came to our dances. Well, at one time I guess there

speaker: TW
words: was another family there. But, oh, gee, they were popular people to

speaker: TW
words: dance with. There was this feeling about a lot of people; some Reser

speaker: TW
words: vation Indians you felt were not as clean physically. And there was

speaker: TW
words: this feeling alright, but the Indians in our neighborhood were- as

speaker: TW
words: far as housekeeping and so forth, the most of the rest of

speaker: TW
words: us. So this would depend. But, yes- you see I was- our family was

speaker: TW
words: real friendly with the few Indians that were around; thought real

speaker: TW
words: highly of them and so we have also heard some of their feelings of

speaker: TW
words: the unfairness of the treatment that they've had. Our sympathies were

speaker: TW
words: with them. I guess there were people that apparently would go out of

speaker: TW
words: their way to make them feel worse for having been Indian- or moreso

speaker: TW
words: than that- now one of our Indian friends, or our half-breed friends,

speaker: TW
words: I should probably say, was telling my mother about how difficult it

speaker: TW
words: was to be of mixed blood because she said, "The White people hate you

speaker: TW
words: and the Indians don't like you." So there was a problem. We, of course,

speaker: TW
words: didn't feel it and we didn't participate in it, but i think you'd have

speaker: TW
words: to say, yes, by and large, like all the, they haven't been

speaker: TW
words: dealt fairly with. I think it's probably better now,

speaker: SS
words: Although I have heard many people say that they felt highly towards

speaker: SS
words: the Indians, that they liked them very much.

speaker: TW
words: My dad did.

speaker: SS
words: I almost wonder whether in some ways there might have been more con

speaker: SS
words: tact in those days between the races-

speaker: TW

words: are when an Indian man and his, three-quarters I guess, son-worked on

speaker: TW
words: the same combine that I did. And they were just a lot of fun. They

words: were fun to work with and nice, and we shared a lot of things that I

words: would be much worse off not to havex for instance, Indian words, Indian

speaker: TW
words: language. They've got a keen sense of humor; real keen, real sharp.

speaker: TW
words: And also, they're quite mechanically inclined. Quite ingenious. And

speaker: TW
words: when it comes to hardship, they're real tough, you know, they're not

speaker: TW
words: complaining and crying about conditions that are tough. They acknowtbor-

speaker: TW
words: ledge it being tough, but it's with a sort of a humor that you .

words: to respect. In the Indian language there aren't any swear words as

speaker: TW
words: we know swear words. But it used to be quite a lot of fun then, insteading

words: of swearing about wind to, use Indian words that had other

words: meanings that still when translated into English would be swearing.

words: And this sort of helped to pass the time.

words: Can you give me an example of that?

speaker: TW
words: Oh, no, it would be hard to bring it out because I'd have to say In

speaker: TW
words: dian words in Indian that it gets so involved translating something

words: that's funny in one language- you had to- it takes the two languages

speaker: TW
words: - to see the joke. Well, I'll give you one little rough joke that we

words: pendage on the end of a word. And that word is often discriptive.

words: a bull, they'll use the word for cow and follow it by "talaheen".

speaker: TW
words: being an Indian word for testicle, meaning testicle. Or as we

words: would say "ball". This animal which possesses balls- that would be the

speaker: TW
words: translation of the Indian word "Talaheen". Well, this was real funny

speaker: TW
words: when we had trouble on the combine to use the Indian sentence that

words: would say, "We have lost our ballbearing." This ended with the word

speaker: TW
words: "talaheen", which means bull! But anyhow the Indians would chuckles

words: over these things all the time, and of course they'd tell us the joke

words: and we would too. And this was the kind of thing that went on! (Laugh)

speaker: SS
words: Talaheen, was bull? There wasn't another word you had to use before

speaker: SS
words: talaheen?

speaker: TW
words: Well, usually they would say of a bull, "Talaheen", you know that

words: was enough, if you're talking about cattle. And it would work- if you're

words: talking about sheep of course, it probably would work just as well, too.

speaker: TW
words: enough

words: Understandably the word for rooster, it's manchicken. Or chicken man.

words: (Chuckles) Because you see, it wouldn't describe a rooster.

words: treatment; that indicates to me that they must have felt pretty good

words: it between people of their own race, too, you know and the Indians are

words: I think mostly as kind of a joke and he sort of felt like he had got it

words: on the Missourian because he had used a worse word than Siwash. And

words: For an Indian to marry a White in someways it status for both.

words: Some ways.

speaker: TW

words: Sffuaw Man is the term that I have heard used.

words: Yeah, that was the thing. I know of a falling-out between two neigh

words: bors that never was healed just because of that. The white man had

speaker: TW
words: called the other white man, who had married an Indian squaw a "Squawman".

speaker: TW
words: Insulting him and insulting his wife, and a pretty serious

speaker: TW
words: thing, too. And it was serious because the man was angry when he

speaker: TW
words: said it. Now, I knew another man who felt a great deal of friendship

speaker: TW
words: for a man that was married to an Indian in the neighborhood

speaker: TW
words: there. So much so-these people were so much accepted that

speaker: TW
words: you had to kind of guard yourself to remember that they were Indians,

speaker: TW
words: and they didn't think of them as Indians, they thought of them dif

speaker: TW
words: ferently. Well, this fellow always referred to his wife as the "Old

speaker: TW
words: Squaw", his own wife as the old squaw, just in a way of speaking, and

speaker: TW
words: he would commonly say to any other white man, "Well, bring your old

speaker: TW
words: squaw over." Get the old squaws together. Well, he said, before he

speaker: TW
words: could stop himself, he said to this man who was married to an Indian

speaker: TW
words: "Well, bring the old squaw over someday, or tomorrow or next day or

speaker: TW
words: something like that." And he said he no more than said it than he just

speaker: TW
words: wished he could die, there was no undoing it. But as far as I knew

speaker: TW
words: or ever heard there never was any resentment by the man who he said it

speaker: TW
words: to because he understood it exactly as it was meant. Well, another

speaker: TW
words: example of how you can get into that kind of a trap; there was a Negro

speaker: TW
words: boy that worked for us and he was with us, in the house with us, and

speaker: TW
words: I liked him, I liked his brother, and he was telling us of a thing

speaker: TW
words: that had happened to him when he was young. He and his brother, they

speaker: TW
words: were raised by their grandfather, their father had left them, abandonded

speaker: TW
words: them, I guess,but he and Stan, this La Verne was the one that

speaker: TW
words: worked for us and Stan was his brother, telling about one time he and

speaker: TW
words: Stan were out and got into some kind of a minor scrape like kids will,

speaker: TW
words: and Stan says, "Well-" Or I said to Stan, "; we tell Grandpa?"

speaker: TW
words: And Stan said, "Hell, no, we're free, white and twenty-one." Well, I

speaker: TW
words: had a hell of a time keeping a straight face! Verne, I don't think

speaker: TW
words: ever noticed that he'd ever said it. Another time we were sitting at

speaker: TW
words: the table and we were talking about something or other that somebody

speaker: TW
words: had done, or some mistake a hired man had made or something like that,

speaker: TW
words: or something that he'd done that he shouldn't have done, and I said

speaker: TW
words: something to the effect, "Well, Verne would certainly never make that

speaker: TW
words: mistake." And my mother said, "Well, Verne is a white man." My mother

speaker: TW
words: never even realized what she'd said. I don't know that Verne did, no

speaker: TW
words: thing was ever said about it. I sure felt embarrassed.

speaker: SS
words: Do you think that those young fellows experienced any problems or any

speaker: SS
words: thing while they were- not from your family, I'm sure, but still, they

speaker: SS
words: would have stood out quite a bit in the community.

speaker: TW
words: Well, La Verne was always welcomeAand treated real well by this In

speaker: TW
words: dian-White family. I think he was treated equ. ally well by others,

speaker: TW
words: ihlat is, the boy,

speaker: TW
words: but he always, I think felt more easy with us ..Negro. felt more

speaker: TW
words: easy with us and this Negro family than he did with some of the others.

speaker: TW
words: Of course, this was back when La Verne- although he was a well-liked

speaker: TW
words: student in high school, and was a top athlete and had lots of friends,

speaker: TW
words: and the family is an old established family; " pioneer family,

speaker: TW
words: yet he couldn't get his hair cut in the daytime in a barbershop in Mos

speaker: TW
words: cow, anyway. And one time he and I were eating in a restaurant in

speaker: TW
words: Coeur d'Alene and he said to me, "You know, if I wasn't with you, they

speaker: TW
words: wouldn't have served me here." And I hadn't realized this you know.

speaker: TW
words: But there were lots of restaurants where he could go. There was a Neg

speaker: TW
words: ro athlete in high school in Lewiston when I went there and this foot

speaker: TW
words: ball team was in a neighboring town and they just treated this Negro

speaker: TW
words: swell. And there was another time, I think it was in a restaurant in

speaker: TW
words: Lewiston where the football team went in to eat and they wouldn't serve

speaker: TW
words: the Negro, so the whole team walked out. And this was not uncommon,

speaker: TW
words: although I hadn't realized it so much so until I had a few of these

speaker: TW
words: brought to my attention.

speaker: SS
words: Was Verne a Lewiston boy?

speaker: TW
words: No, he was Moscow.

speaker: SS
words: What's the last name?

speaker: TW
words: Settles.

speaker: SS
words: Oh!

speaker: TW
words: You know Gene Settles.

speaker: SS
words: Yeah, I know Gene Pretty well.

speaker: TW
words: He was Gene's nephew. He was at the memorial services this last De

speaker: TW
words: coration Day, Verne was. Of course, the Settles, people don't think

speaker: TW
words: of them as being Negro, you know. An awful lot of people don't. Even

speaker: TW
words: the people that are- people that discriminate, or think they do, or

speaker: TW
words: feel they do, think of the Settles as being a little different.

speaker: TW
words: Which I probably is true because they are intelligent people

speaker: TW
words: and they adjusted. Now the Settles, as little boys, went to school

speaker: TW
words: with Elizabeth's brothers. But, I don't know, things like that, they're

speaker: TW
words: getting better and better all the time I think.

speaker: SS
words: I talked to Gene at length about what it was like for his family back

speaker: SS
words: in those days and he has a great deal of pride.

speaker: TW
words: Yeah. They are people that are intelligent enough, you know, to make

speaker: TW
words: a lot of room for other people, and overlook as nearly as you can

speaker: TW
words: slights and so on, 'cause I'm sure they must have felt them one place

speaker: TW
words: and another. I was with La Verne in Coeur d'Alene one time, besides

speaker: TW
words: this restaurant that I mentioned where nothing bad happened, this was

speaker: TW
words: during wartime- there was an army officer from the South someplace who

speaker: TW
words: really went out of his way to be nasty to La Verne, a perfect stranger.

speaker: SS
words: World War II?

speaker: TW
words: Uh-huh.

speaker: SS
words: You know when Gene became the warehouse superintendent for Grain Gro

speaker: SS
words: wers, he said to me that it surprised a lot of people and someone else

speaker: SS
words: told me, who's husband was on the board at that time for the Grain Gro

speaker: SS
words: wers, that he was fit to be tied at the time when that happened. He

speaker: SS
words: was quite disturbed thinking thinking it was very. But to

speaker: SS
words: me the fact that Gene got that position is very interesting because he

speaker: SS
words: was in charge of a lot of white men.

speaker: TW
words: Yeah, and probably Gene being the kind of a man he is, probably the

speaker: TW
words: people that worked with and under him resented him less than the one

speaker: TW
words: that you speak of.

speaker: SS
words: Yeah.

speaker: TW
words: Because they were nice people. They were just nice people.

speaker: SS
words: And I think independent because of the fact that they were farmers.

speaker: SS
words: Do you think that made a difference?

speaker: TW
words: Well, you went to the home where Gene was raised, or that is, his

speaker: TW
words: parents later got, it was one of the nicest homes in Moscow; one of

speaker: TW
words: the nicest kept up and neat and clean. And Stanley, La Verne's brother

speaker: TW
words: he had manners. They just made me ashamed of myself, I mean, he was

speaker: TW
words: really polished.

speaker: SS
words: One of the things I want to ask you about was the change that went on

speaker: SS
words: during- through time, as the farms began to become larger and as the

speaker: SS
words: families grew up, what I'm thinking now is the problem that I sense

speaker: SS
words: was by the fact that only a limited number of the boys could stay on

speaker: SS
words: the farm and become the operators. Seems like things like things were

speaker: SS
words: going that way.

speaker: TW
words: Yes, I think that's probably a problem. Of course in our family, our

speaker: TW
words: family - generation after generation got smaller and smaller, so

speaker: TW
words: that it just fitted very well. But I have neighbors who had several

speaker: TW
words: sons on the farm and of course they either got to buy and acquire more

speaker: TW
words: land if they all want to be farmers or else some of them got to decide

speaker: TW
words: not to be farmers. One of the boys that worked for me for several

speaker: TW
words: years,^through most of his highschool and most of his college years,

speaker: TW
words: he is one of three or four boys, I guess, not counting any of the girls,

speaker: TW
words: and he took up agricultural engineering. And I don't know what his ol

speaker: TW
words: der brother did, but it wasn't farming, but I think the younger son

speaker: TW
words: has been a farmer. Might very well be though that the younger son

speaker: TW
words: won ft farm either. His dad is still doing the farming and he's a

speaker: TW
words: man about my age.

speaker: TW
words: When did the farms start to reduce in number? Began to be larger and

speaker: TW
words: more consolidated?

speaker: TW
words: Oh, it's been going on since before the first homesteads were proyed

speaker: TW
words: up on. Lot of people would come in and homestead a piece of land just

speaker: TW
words: for the sake of selling out their rights before they proved up on the

speaker: TW
words: homestead. And I think it's a process that went on right from the be

speaker: TW
words: ginning. My dad operated his mother's farm, had an outfit- or his fa

speaker: TW
words: ther's farm- had an outfit to operate with, and he had several brothers

speaker: TW
words: and so he had some source of labor; paid for it, of course. But he

speaker: TW
words: just merely bought up land around there as opportunity presented itself

speaker: TW
words: and bought the land and really accumulated more land than any of his

speaker: TW
words: brothers, or all of 'em put together, for that matter. And never got

speaker: TW
words: any free land. But, some of the first land he bought, I think he got

speaker: TW
words: for in the neighborhood of, oh, somewhere between sixteen and twenty

speaker: TW
words: dollars an acre. But you must remember, that in 1932 or '33 some of

speaker: TW
words: that land right down there sold for about thirty-four dollars an acre.

speaker: TW
words: That's Depression time. And some sold for forty. In 1945 some choice

speaker: TW
words: land south of Moscow sold for the unheard of price, the sky high price

speaker: TW
words: of fifty dollars an acre. And that's within three miles of Moscow.

speaker: TW
words: And now, you hear of a man selling for $1,500 an acre. So what's land

speaker: TW
words: worth, is more of a question of what's money worth. Seems like anytime

speaker: TW
words: you buy land you gotta pay more than it looks like it would pay out.

speaker: TW
words: And if you don't buy land you haven't got security because you can

speaker: TW
words: lose your lease in a hurry.

speaker: SS
words: That's something that in fact, Gene Settle talked to me about. Gene

speaker: SS
words: about other people that in the '20ls as people

speaker: SS
words: Settle talked to me

speaker: SS
words: started going to gas machinery that there was a big squeeze on renters

speaker: SS
words: who didn't have it; had horses.

speaker: TW
words: Uh-huh. I think probably I should have answered your question this

speaker: TW
words: way; of course, the big incentive toward owning more ]and had to

speaker: TW
words: come when you had easier ways of farming, of course with the tractors

speaker: TW
words: and with the advanced machinery and so on. And with the acquiring of

speaker: TW
words: capital, too; the ability to finance purchase of this kind of machinery

speaker: TW
words: when it became available.

speaker: SS
words: In your family, when did the tractors first—

speaker: TW
words: We bought our first tractor in 1930. And it was owned by my father

speaker: TW
words: and a brother in partnership, who still used horses at the same time,

speaker: TW
words: too.

speaker: SS
words: Did the tractor make a big difference in

speaker: TW
words: Yeah, yeah. You could, you could not only farm more land but you

speaker: TW
words: could farm it faster and better, do a better job of it. Produce more.

speaker: TW
words: Well, one thing, you think of the land you had to devote to just pro

speaker: TW
words: ducing feed for the horses thatiyou worked; took quite a lot of that.

speaker: TW
words: And doing your work at the right time made a lot of difference. Another

speaker: TW
words: thing, they were always short of horses when needed them

speaker: TW
words: and had too many when ' didn't have any work for them. Tractor,

speaker: TW
words: when you get done in the fall you just put it in the shed. The horses,

speaker: TW
words: you got to work for them all winter. Of course, a lot of the year,

speaker: TW
words: they could gather their own feed if you were lucky enough to have some

speaker: TW
words: inexpensive pasture land, that would help you winter them over. That

speaker: TW
words: was really a pretty good thing. the horses, too, dry feed

speaker: TW
words: isn't the best way to maintain animals all the time either.

speaker: SS
words: Now, before I go, I wanted to ask you-It's something that Mary Edwards

speaker: SS
words: said to me about your mother, that after your brother that was spastic

speaker: SS
words: was born, that she really had to devote a great deal of her effort

speaker: SS
words: to take care of him.

speaker: TW
words: Yeah, yeah, she did. But of course, you must understand that he was

speaker: TW
words: able to do quite a few things. He learned to drive a car. He could

speaker: TW
words: take her anyplace she wanted to go and he kept the books, the records

speaker: TW
words: for the operation there and did the banking; ran a lot of the errands.

speaker: TW
words: But he had to be dressed and put to bed and cleaned and cared for, you

speaker: TW
words: know. And I have never heard her complain about it though.

speaker: SS
words: It seems like the kind of thing that would make you have more compas

speaker: SS
words: sion for other people to have that in your own family.

speaker: TW
words: Well, yes, you do. But there's also- there's something else that goes

speaker: TW
words: along with it too, and IAwould be dishonest if I didn't admit it, but

speaker: TW
words: I think probably most people would too- you have this compassion, al

speaker: TW
words: right, but also it takes any pleasure that you might otherwise haveit

speaker: TW
words: kind of destroys it for you, that the less fortunate one can't en

speaker: TW
words: joy it with you. Or also, there's a feeling of guilt in any pleasuresay

speaker: TW
words: it's me- any pleasure that I might have there's always a feeling

speaker: TW
words: of guilt that accompanies that. Still, you have- there's a lo t of

speaker: TW
words: things that you feel that you ou ght to have a right to do without

speaker: TW
words: being handicapped by the fact that your brother is a cripple. Why

speaker: TW
words: should I be crippled, just because my brother is crippled? Why should

speaker: TW
words: I act as a cripple? Why should I have to take it everyplace I go?

speaker: TW
words: And yet, you feel guilty if you don't. Now there's this aspect of it

speaker: TW
words: too. So I don't know what kind of an answer to give you on that. I

speaker: TW
words: think probably I'm compassionate in a way and still having that ex

speaker: TW
words: perience, I think it's almost inevitable that maybe you develop a

speaker: TW
words: sort of a numbness or a lack of conscience. You also have this feeling

speaker: TW
words: of guilt that goes along with it, too. So I don't know. You look back

speaker: TW
words: you know, now that he's dead,Athen you think of all the things thatlittle

speaker: TW
words: sacrifices that I might have made and made it better for him

speaker: TW
words: and you feel ashamed that you didn't. And yet, were X back there, I

speaker: TW
words: doubt if I would have the character to act any better than I did. I

speaker: TW
words: don't know I can explain it any better than that or not. But that

speaker: TW
words: is a part of it.

speaker: SS
words: You probably had a pretty strong feeling of protecting him.

speaker: TW
words: Oh, yes, and it starts from before you can even remember. Oh, you

speaker: TW
words: fight with a crippled brother the same as you'd have to fight with any

speaker: TW
words: other brother, they're no more of an angel than you are, either. Maybe

speaker: TW
words: not as much. He was pretty good. He was never spoiled, you know

speaker: TW
words: because of being unfortunate. I thought this was a pretty difficult

speaker: TW
words: thing for my parents to do, but they tried to be as just to the well

speaker: TW
words: kids as they would be to the crippled one. And of course, the crippled

speaker: TW
words: kid has to be punished once in a while, too. that must be pretty

speaker: TW
words: tough on a parent. Oh, we had lots of fun together, too. Lots of

speaker: TW
words: things to enjoy. He was generous, very generous, brother.

speaker: SS
words: Mary Edwards really remembers that- as your mother being the ideal

speaker: SS
words: homemaker and housekeeper. She feels that your house was the most out

speaker: SS
words: standing one of all of 'em, as far as how clean it was and how nice it

speaker: SS
words: was and the feeling the atmosphere, and that's what she remembers.

speaker: TW
words: Did Mary ever tell you how she keeps her house? Well, I imagine that

speaker: TW
words: she put my mother to shame, really, her house is splendid. I think

speaker: TW
words: kids sort of pick an ideal older person to sort of pattern themselves

speaker: TW
words: after. And speaking of my mother the way she did, why, I really think

speaker: TW
words: that if my mother was her example she outdid her example.^ Lovely

speaker: TW
words: housekeeper! Always neat. Always made you welcome in her house, too.

speaker: TW
words: The same as my mother.

speaker: SS
words: Do you think that that was in itself a real important value with

speaker: SS
words: people at that time? The pride in the kind of house they kept?

speaker: TW
words: Housekeeping? Oh, yes, it was a sort of a plume that a lot of people

speaker: TW
words: held up too, because it was quite difficult. And now just to show you

speaker: TW
words: how difficult; you've heard Mary describe the kind of a housekeeper

speaker: TW
words: my mother was; well, I overhauled an automobile on the floor of her

speaker: TW
words: livingroom one winter! So she didn't sacrifice everything to the house

speaker: TW
words: keeping. And manys the time we've had a baby pig in the house to thaw

speaker: TW
words: out. All the chickens that we dressed, you know, part of the dressing

speaker: TW
words: was done in the house on the kitchen table. And so, the house is part

speaker: TW
words: of the farm, the farmhouse is, and still Mother managed to keep a pret

speaker: TW
words: ty nice house. And she took quite a pride when she was young enough

speaker: TW
words: and strong enough that when the men came in from the field that the

speaker: TW
words: table looked nice and there was a white tablecloth on the table and

speaker: TW
words: the table was set nice. Everything was right. Yeah, I think that's

speaker: TW
words: right; I think the pioneer women took quite a pride in their house.

speaker: TW
words: It was the only thing they had really, was their house. Oh, of course

speaker: TW
words: people took pride in the horses too.

speaker: SS
words: But women, maybe more the house.

speaker: TW
words: More the house. The house was the woman's. They weren't very many

speaker: TW
words: of 'em able to keep much of a lawn or yard, you know. They would like

speaker: TW
words: to, but there were very few pressure water systems, and by pressure

speaker: TW
words: I mean gravity water systems. Windmills,of course you had water when

speaker: TW
words: the wind blew, and you pumped it and carried it in a bucket when it

speaker: TW
words: didn't.

speaker: SS
words: Were they common, windmills, at that time?

speaker: TW
words: No, I would say they were a little less than common.* Most people

speaker: TW
words: carried their water in buckets.

speaker: SS
words: What you're saying is, really it was a struggle for a woman to be

speaker: SS
words: able to keep a house clean for lack of water.

speaker: TW
words: Uh-huh. Now my mother was a little bit lucky in this respect, she

speaker: TW
words: had lived in town soem and her father was a tinner and a pretty good

speaker: TW
words: plumber, and my dad was pretty ingenious, too, and so as far back as

speaker: TW
words: I can remember there was a water system in every house that I lived

speaker: TW
words: in, although it wasn't always in working order. v. there was plum

speaker: TW
words: bing there inside.plumbing. Of course part of that might have been

speaker: TW
words: because my brother was crippled. But we also had outdoor plumbing,

speaker: TW
words: too. Because everybody did.

speaker: SS
words: Well, I guess I had better get going it's nearly 10:30.

speaker: TW
words: I don't think I have helped you much.

speaker: TW
words: I always thought of this country where I first discovered myself as

speaker: TW
words: being an old, established country and the older I get, and I look b ack

speaker: TW
words: and get a better perspective on iy, I see how close to the edge of

speaker: TW
words: the pioneering I was in. Yeah, when I was born was.thirty-one years

speaker: TW
words: after the Wahl people had moved into this country, and I'm sixty-five

speaker: TW
words: years old. But when I was about ten years old, it seemed to me like-

speaker: TW
words: I thought to myself it must have been going on about like that forever!

speaker: TW
words: And still, I can remember fairly respectable caravans of Indians coming

speaker: TW
words: by our place on the way to the mountains to pick huckleberries, or

speaker: TW
words: whatever they went there for. I don't remember ever seeing any wild

speaker: TW
words: horses, but I suppose the last of 'em must have been around somewhere

speaker: TW
words: very shortly before I was born. Maybe were still here.
